I believe #141 might resolve your problem. Until that's added, another thing you could try is the following.

- type `tty` in a terminal. It will output something like  `/dev/pts/0`
- open gdbgui in another terminal
- in gdbgui's terminal widget, type `tty /dev/pts/0`. Now all i/o is done through that terminal.

Read more: https://sourceware.org/gdb/onlinedocs/gdb/Input_002fOutput.html

You'll have to do that yourself, it doesn't make good sense for `pew` to do that for you. You'll just want to write some stuff in your bash profile to look at the `VIRTUAL_ENV` environment variable. In mine, I just have it checking whether there's anything set for it, and it puts a unicode snake character (🐍) in the prompt, so that I know I'm using a virtualenv (it doesn't tell me which one).

Because I'm using fish shell, it probably won't help you much in figuring out bash, but here it is if it does:

```fish
function fish_prompt
  if [ -n "$VIRTUAL_ENV" ]
    echo -n '🐍 '
  end
  echo -n '🐟  '
end
```

The case where select and send disagree hints at the one time where  TCP_NOTSENT_LOWAT could hurt -- if the application already has the data, and sending it to the kernel now will let the app do some cleanup (or exit, or further progress while the CPU is otherwise idle).  That said, using asynchronous output for an app like this is enough of a corner case that  TCP_NOTSENT_LOWAT should probably be the default.

This was called differently in the past, but nowadays pixiv calls it "Like".
You can like each post once a day. It sort of acts like a vote and pixiv uses the votes each post aquired in the last 24 hours to do a global ranking against all other posts.

The thing is: This "Like" counter does not reset. Yes, you can vote only once per day for a single post and this vote is only counted for that day, but the counter you see in the picture will never decrease.

I just wanted to point this out to remove any misunderstandings from the topic.

And this is what i actually want: An API endpoint to like posts. As far as i remember i was able to do it in the mobile app.

是的，我也发现了我的错误。我比较常用PAPI（比较旧的版本）而不是AAPI，在PAPI里才需要额外获取。你可以参考这段代码。

```python
from pprint import pprint

from pixivpy3 import AppPixivAPI


def main():
    aapi = AppPixivAPI()
    aapi.login('username', 'password')

    json_result = aapi.illust_ranking('day', date='2018-07-20')  # No.6 contains multi pages

    for illust in json_result.illusts[3:6]:
        if illust.page_count == 1 and illust.type != 'ugoira':  # single page illust
            image_urls = [
                illust.meta_single_page.original_image_url
            ]
        elif illust.page_count > 1:  # multi page illust
            image_urls = [
                page.image_urls.original
                for page in illust.meta_pages
            ]
        else:  # ugoira
            image_urls = []  # AAPI will not provide download URL of ugoira. Use PAPI.

        pprint(image_urls)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

but a library shall not make assumptions on how a user utilizes stdout.
warnings is a friendlier way to issue informative notes from a library.

the `contextlib` module from the stdlib has a `closing()` context manager helper which might help here:

```python
from contextlib import closing

with closing(plyvel.DB('./_db') as db:
  print db.get('key')
```

alternatively you could write a context manager wrapper if you want more flexibility and less repetition in your code (e.g. no repeated db names). easiest way is to use `contextlib` again:

```python
from contextlib import contextmanager

@contextmanager
def magic_db():
    db = plyvel.DB('./_db')
    try:
        yield db
    finally:
        db.close()
```

...which can be used like this:

```python
with magic_db() as db:
    db.get(b'foo')
```

I'll have to clarify the docs and release notes. It's the `BedTool`'s iterator that wraps `pysam.AlignmentFile`, not the `BedTool` itself. Below is how to use it.

I suppose I could open a pysam.AlignmentFile automatically upon instantiating a BedTool object and expose that via pysam_bamfile, but I'd prefer not to since that would likely leave open filehandles laying around. This way, a filehandle is only opened when you're iterating over it.

```python
import pybedtools

# x is a BedTool object, independent of file type
x = pybedtools.example_bedtool('x.bam')

for i in x:
    # For consistency, when iterating over x, we get Intervals --
    # same as any other file type
    break

# But underneath, it's iterating over a pysam.AlignmentFile
# and converting to Interval objects. If we want that AlignmentFile directly,
# we need to convert it to an iterator first
y = iter(x)

# y is now a pybedtools.bedtool.BAM object
y.pysam_bamfile
```

`get_string_at_rva` actually returns a `bytes` type, which is an array of int. To use it as a string, you will have to call `.decode('ASCII')` on the result. (The PE spec mandates ASCII encoding for most if not all strings, the exception being the "Unicode" strings in the version info, which are UTF-16. I worked on this last week. Essentially, the only issue that arose from using the 3.6 binaries with mlaunch was that if you pass in a hostname when starting a replica set or a sharded cluster, you will get an error when they attempt to start talking with each other as a result of [MongoDB binaries binding to local host by default](https://docs.mongodb.com/master/release-notes/3.6-compatibility/#localhost-binding-compatibility-changes). As a result of this change, connections from other nodes will be refused by a `mongod` or a `mongos` only when a non localhost hostname is used. 

**Replica Set**
mlaunch.py --replica set --name repl1 --nodes 3 --hostname Stephens-MacBook-Pro.local


launching: mongod on port 27017
launching: mongod on port 27018
launching: mongod on port 27019
replSetInitiate quorum check failed because not all proposed set members responded affirmatively: Stephens-MacBook-Pro.local:27018 failed with Connection refused, Stephens-MacBook-Pro.local:27019 failed with Connection refused  - will retry


**Sharded Cluster**

launching: mongod on port 27018
launching: mongod on port 27019
launching: mongod on port 27020
launching: mongod on port 27021
launching: mongod on port 27022
launching: mongod on port 27023
launching: mongod on port 27024
launching: mongod on port 27025
launching: mongod on port 27026
launching: config server on port 27027

replica set 'configRepl' initialized.

replSetInitiate quorum check failed because not all proposed set members responded affirmatively: Stephens-MacBook-Pro.local:27019 failed with Connection refused, Stephens-MacBook-Pro.local:27020 failed with Connection refused  - will retry

**The Fix**
The way to address this is by passing in either a valid `bind_ip` list or by specifying `bind_ip_all` options at runtime. Initially, I was going to propose passing in `bind_ip_all` anytime a hostname is used but this is a direct contradiction to what the localhost binding feature of 3.6 is all about. Instead I created a series of checks in the `def _construct_mongod` and `def _construct_mongos` functions that will:
1. Check if a non-localhost name is being passed
2. Check that the binary version is 3.6 or above
3. Check that the replica set or sharded cluster options are being used
4. Check if no `bind_ip` options have been passed 

If these conditions are met, then it will raise a system exit and throw an error message: 

_If hostname is specified, please include '--bind_ip_all' or '--bind_ip' options when deploying replica sets or sharded cluster with MongoDB version 3.6.0 or greater_

It will also clear the data path of any for any mongod/mongos instances that were spun up.

Well, technically a `np.float64` is a `float`, in the `isinstance` sense:
```Python
>>> a = np.float64(3.3)
>>> type(a)
numpy.float64
>>> isinstance(a, float)
True
```
In order to stay sane I usually make the assumption that `np.float64` can be used interchangeably with `float`.  Code which doesn't do make this assumption bums me out but does exist (Theano is a good example).  I don't think it would do any harm to be more explicit about casting things to `float`, if that would fix the issue you're experiencing.  But, I'd also gently suggest that the other code you're using which considers `np.float64` and `float` different should maybe be changed too.

hey, i merged it in so it would stop "rotting" on side branches, but i'd love if you could cleanup (or explain the reason for) the following: * why is there need for a module hack? why not ``from plumbum.color import fg, bg`` and then ``fg.blue | bg.red``, where ``fg`` and ``bg`` are perfectly normal modules? * why is there more than "one obvious way to do it (TM)"? e.g., why ``+`` and ``-`` and ``[]`` and ``<<``` and context managers and "stick shift" all in one? i find it very confusing and too error prone. * subtracting styles is a bad practice, i.e., HTML vs XHTML. while supported by HTML4, ``foobar`` was deprecated and went away in HTML5. you don't change style *that* often in order to be *that* efficient. here's a snippet from your example: ```python with colors: print('It is always a good idea to be in a context manager, to avoid being', 'left with a colorsed terminal if there is an exception!') print(colors.bold + "This is bold and exciting!" - colors.bold) print(colors.bg.cyan + "This is on a cyan background." + colors.reset) print(colors.fg[42] + "If your terminal supports 256 colorss, this is colorsful!" + colors.reset) print() for c in colors: print(c + u'\u2588', end='') colors.reset() print() print('Colors can be reset ' + colors.underline['Too!']) for c in colors[:16]: print(c["This is in colors!"]) ``` * i find it awkward that ``colors`` is an iterable (i.e., except for the example using it here, where would anyone *iterate* through all possible colors? or the first 16 colors? what does that even mean? is there a first color? ANSI color numbers are just arbitrary) * also, i find it strange that the module is a context manager on its own. i understand the need for cleanup on exceptions, but that's what ``atexit`` is for. when you first import the module, it could register an ``atexit`` callback that just resets color. simpler, cleaner and doesn't require the user to be not forget doing it. * hex colors are nice, but would rarely ever be used. i'd just have ``fd.rgb`` and ``bg.rgb`` functions that take either (r,g,b) or "#rrggbb" and return the appropriate color objects. * also, naming so many colors seems superfluous (e.g., who would ever use ``deep_pink_4``?) but since you already did the hard work i'm fine with that. here's a simplified, cleaner version that i have in mind: ```python from plumbum.colors import fg, bg, bold, underline # or ``from plumbum.colors.fd import red`` if that's all you want with bg.blue: print("hello ", fg.red["world"], " bingo was his ", underline["name-o"]) yell = fg.red | underline | bold print(yell["SHAKIRA SHKIRA"]) print("and ", fg.red["it can ", bold["be nested ", underline["just as well"]]]) ``` thanks for your hard work, man, i really appreciate it. but here i think you should let usability guide you (i.e., "the lean startup" approach) and not "think in advance" what would be cool (to be able) to do. on the other hand, i think the library is missing some built-in semantic combinators such as ``warn``, ``title``, ``fatal``, ``highlight``. that's what your end users would most probably want in the end of the day, i.e., a "CSS layer" on top of the low level. looking at the evolution of HTML is really helpful here.

Hi,
Thanks.
Actually, this is expected behaviour - although I'll be the first to admit that it is not very obvious. The problem with the first case is that you end up referring to memory that belonged to a temporary. This occurs in the special case of non-reference return values, and there is a section in the documentation where I've attempted to explain it - but perhaps not sufficiently clearly. The short story is that you should use `ndarray_copy()` rather than `ndarray()` when returning values (non-references) from a C++ function. This will ensure that a copy is made of the underlying data. The second case works because eigency is able to automatically detect that you are referring to a temporary (an r-value reference), and automatically makes a copy in this case.

### The long story
The `ndarray` function is defined like this:
```c++
template <typename Derived>
inline PyArrayObject *ndarray(Eigen::PlainObjectBase<Derived> &m) {
```
This means that it cannot differentiate between a c++ function returning a reference and a function returning a value, because they will both match this function definition. If the code is compiled with a c++11 compliant compiler, another definition is made available:
```c++
template <typename Derived>
inline PyArrayObject *ndarray(Eigen::PlainObjectBase<Derived> &&m) {
```
which will detect the case from your version2 example, and automatically make a copy. One could argue that making a copy should be the default (so that errors like your's don't occur), but that would mean that there are cases where you get a copy when you expected a view). As a guiding principle in Eigency, we reuse memory whenever possible, which is why it works like it does. However, you can always override these defaults by calling `ndarray_view` or `ndarray_copy` explicitly.

Bug: when the limit is reached we closed active tasks, but didn't clear the queue and so the test continued.

```python
# api.py
def _done(self):
    if self._isDone:
        return
    self._isDone = True
    self._to_check.clear() # clear the queue <= added in v0.1.2
    for f in self._on_check:
        if not f.cancelled():
            f.cancel()
    self._tasks.cancel()
    self.push_to_result(None)
    log.debug('Done!')
```

**Tip**: use search by key instead of the `expectedType` property (my fault; not marked as private - this is an experimental):
```python
# bad
if proxy.expectedType == 'H':
    ptype = 'http'
elif proxy.expectedType == 'S':
    ptype = 'socks5'

# good
if 'HTTP' in proxy.types:
    ptype = 'http'
elif 'SOCKS5' in proxy.types:
    ptype = 'socks5'
```

**Just in case some further information:**
If we remove the infinite loop (server3.2.py.issue.txt, line 54: `while True:`), then the execution time will depend on the number of live proxy. In our case, to find 2 live proxies among 22,5 k takes on average 5-15 seconds. Why is such difference? Because proxies are stored in an unordered collection (set)), so the order of proxy check is different every run.

In case we use providers instead of raw data, the execution time takes 2-4 seconds.

@Enderlein It is better to make PRs from a different branch than your master branch because then if you want to open another PR simultaneously, it will be easier.

The following git command will make a new branch (Make sure you are in `master` branch).
```bash
git checkout -b <branch name>
```
To change branches,
```
git checkout <branch name>
```


If you want to update your fork using command line, use
```bash
git remote add upstream https://github.com/vn-ki/anime-downloader.git  # Adds a remote named upstream, has to be done only once
git remote update  # Gets changes from all remotes
git rebase -i upstream/master  # Rebase instead of merge so that git history is clean
git push -f  # force push because of rebase
```

The check is at least somewhat needed because several of the calculations can break if it goes beyond the `max_value` (getting a progress bar that's wider than your screen for example). But it might be a good idea to allow updating the `max_value` if needed when updating.

For what it's worth, for the time being (and old versions) there is a simple workaround available. Simply override the `max_value` in the loop and you're done:

```python
bar = progressbar.ProgressBar(max_value=10)
bar.start()
for i in range(100):
    if i >= bar.max_value:
        bar.max_value = i
        bar.update(i)
```

Especially for iterators it would be logical if it would skip this check so that's definitely a good idea :)

The *follow *argument tells reversion which objects belong in the same
logical revision. When any object is saved, the *follow* argument will be
used to traverse database relations to save a copy of the related object in
the revision.

relations should be deleted during the revert.

There's a minimum size face it looks for so that the execution time is reasonable. Your sample image is pretty low resolution so most of the the faces are too tiny for the default settings and aren't found.

You can control this using the `number_of_times_to_upsample` parameter. If you change it to `number_of_times_to_upsample=2`, it will upscale the image more and find smaller faces but take longer to run.

For example, this code:

```python
face_locations = face_recognition.face_locations(image, number_of_times_to_upsample=2, model="cnn")
```

... finds 130 faces in the image but can take a long time to run.

But a better solution is to work with higher-resolution images that don't have such low-resolution faces.

I think the way disconnects are handled are very consistent. Basically what I tried to do is prevent is that you send an event from the disconnect handler to the user that got disconnected, as that will obviously fail. There are several ways to trigger a disconnection, and as I said above, they aren't handled in a consistent way. Examples:

- If the server initiates a disconnection, then it is okay to send an event to the disconnected client. Because of the order of the two functions, when the disconnect handler is called, the client is still connected.
- If the client initiates a disconnection by closing the browser or tab, then when the disconnect handler is called the client is already gone, so any attempts to send that client something will fail. I prevent this by removing the client before the disconnect handler is invoked.
- When the client does a clean disconnect by calling the disconnect function (instead of leaving the page), the current code is inconsistent, as it calls the disconnect handler before removing the client. If anything, I would think this case needs to be changed to work like the previous one.

> so in original order, I actually can not do anything related to socket in disconnect function.

This part I don't understand. What do you want to do? You do get the sid of the client. If you broadcast a message, all other clients will receive it, but not the disconnected one, since that one is already gone. Why is that a problem?

You either want to use the global keyword, like this:
```
global UNENCRYPTED_SUFFIX
UNENCRYPTED_SUFFIX = args.unencrypted_suffix
```
, or just set `GPG_EXEC = os.environ.get('SOPS_GPG_EXEC', 'gpg')` on the global scope instead of inside main().

The bug comes from a system limit of how many file streams can be opened. I tried to dig into the snakebite code to figure out exactly where it happens. I think it's in the thrift communication. The problem is that each client opens a new communication pipe, so having too many open at once hits the limit. If every HdfsTarget has the same client, they don't open multiple pipes. The following code does not trigger the bug:
```
from luigi.contrib.hdfs import HdfsTarget, get_autoconfig_client

fs = get_autoconfig_client()
many_targets = [HdfsTarget('/', fs=fs) for _ in range(2000)]
all(target.exists() for target in many_targets)
```
The bug also does not get triggered if there's a chance to garbage collect between exist calls, which usually happens during normal luigi operation. It's only when you hold more HdfsTargets than your system limit on open files in memory at the same time and operate on them all that there's a problem.

I'd like to propose actually pulling in: https://github.com/boto/boto3/issues/220#issuecomment-171477361

Here's what I'm thinking:

* We (botocore) don't need PyOpenSSL to make any API calls.  However, urllib3 will automatically use PyOpenSSL if it's installed in your virtualenv (or even globally if you're not running in a venv).
* Customers want to use PyOpenSSL for other purposes.  i.e, if they're using PyOpenSSL to connect to endpoints that require SNI (API gateway, cloudfront distributions, etc).
* Disabling the use of pyopenssl only for botocore API calls allows customers to still have PyOpenSSL installed in their venv.

Regarding API gateway:  As far as I know, SNI (and therefore PyOpenSSL) is not required for their control plane (i.e any operation from `session.create_client('apigateway')`).  The APIs you *create* from API gateway require SNI.  So disabling the use of PyOpenSSL only within botocore should still allow users to connect to API gateway created APIs, provided that they're not using `botocore.vendored.requests.get(...)` or `botocore.vendored.requests.packages.urllib3` , which people should *not* be doing.

We still need to dig into another issue with S3, newly created buckets, and bytestring payloads, but this will fix all the parity issues we keep seeing with PyOpenSSL.

Are there any downsides to this approach?  Is there anything I'm overlooking?

To me this reads like a question, even though it's stated that it's an issue. Maybe it's just semantics.
Any kind of synchronization among workers is something your application would have to do in some way. GitPython has no ability to wait for a lock to be released.
Generally it is advised to only use one GitPython instance per git repository.
Fixing this appears out of scope for this project, as the lock issue you run into is handled by the git program, not GitPython.
The latter could possibly be configured in some way by sneaking in the [`BlockingLockFile`](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/search?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=BlockingLockFile&type=), but there is no way to do that for the git program itself, as far as I know.

Even though I am closing this question, please feel free to leave more comments or remarks as you see fit.

The code in `pkg_resources` [explicitly allow pre-releases](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/pkg_resources/__init__.py#L3034). Changing it will impact users of the API. I think it would make sense to only allow a pre-release if a requirement does ask for it, something along those lines:

```diff
 setuptools/package_index.py | 9 ++++++++-
 1 file changed, 8 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)

diff --git i/setuptools/package_index.py w/setuptools/package_index.py
index ad743307..4f2a93bf 100755
--- i/setuptools/package_index.py
+++ w/setuptools/package_index.py
@@ -21,7 +21,7 @@ import setuptools
 from pkg_resources import (
     CHECKOUT_DIST, Distribution, BINARY_DIST, normalize_path, SOURCE_DIST,
     Environment, find_distributions, safe_name, safe_version,
-    to_filename, Requirement, DEVELOP_DIST, EGG_DIST,
+    to_filename, Requirement, DEVELOP_DIST, EGG_DIST, parse_version
 )
 from setuptools import ssl_support
 from distutils import log
@@ -620,6 +620,13 @@ class PackageIndex(Environment):
             # Find a matching distribution; may be called more than once
 
             for dist in env[req.key]:
+                if parse_version(dist.version).is_prerelease and not req.specifier.prereleases:
+                    if dist not in skipped:
+                        self.warn(
+                            "Skipping pre-release version: %s", dist,
+                        )
+                        skipped[dist] = 1
+                    continue
 
                 if dist.precedence == DEVELOP_DIST and not develop_ok:
                     if dist not in skipped:
```

You've bumped up against limitations in `easy_install`. It's not viable to use easy_install to upgrade packages installed by other installers such as pip or Debian apt (known to setuptools as externally-managed). What you're getting is corrupted environment where both versions of setuptools are installed, but Python import semantics are allowing the older pkg_resources to take precedence. Here's what's happening:

1. The easy_install upgrade has added setuptools 36.7.2 to your site-packages, but left setuptools 28.8.0 installed as externally-managed packages.
1. `easy_install` command imports `pkg_resources`, which uses Python's import semantics and since [setuptools 25](http://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/history.html#v25-0-0), eggs no longer get precedence. So the pkg_resources from setuptools 28.8.0 gets imported.
1. pkg_resources loads `setuptools 36.7.2` as indicated in the `easy_install` script and invokes the command. Presumably, this gives precedence now to the 36.7.2 package before `setuptools` is imported, so `setuptools` module comes from 36.7.2.
1. When setuptools from 36.7.2 attempts to import something from `pkg_resourecs`, because it was imported in in step (2) from 28.8.0, it doesn't have the module that setuptools expects and requires.

The recommendation here is to not use `easy_install` to install anything. Instead, use `pip install -U setuptools`.

Hey @mikhail. Thanks for bringing up a good question. I've investigated and there's a few pieces. 

The first thing is that the `v2` API doesn't always support parameters even if they're permitted via the `Entity` base class. In this case, `id` for `find_one` is passing through the `id` parameter but the API itself doesn't use it. That's why the `id` field lookup fails. There's no filtering done locally because it would be pretty hard to do that in a non-specific way. Although we could expose a hook to allow it on a per-entity-basis.

The second thing is that the thing you are getting back in your `Service` instance isn't actually the full `EscalationPolicy` it is the `EscalationPolicyReference`. Which includes `summary` which will be identical to name. You can see the schema for it on the v2 docs, the summary field is:

>A short-form, server-generated string that provides succinct, important information about an object suitable for primary labeling of an entity in a client. In many cases, this will be identical to `name`, though it is not intended to be an identifier.

And `EscalationPolicies` only have `id`, `summary`, `type`, `self`, `html_url` fields.

In this case the `TRANSLATE_QUERY_PARAM = ('name',)` turns the `name=` keyword argument into `query=` transparently on the `EscalationPolicy` class methods. A solution could be to have similar translations available or hooks. Also overriding the methods themselves is an option if it's too specific.

I do also think that there's some room for improvement around the `find(...)` and `find_one(...)` semantics. `find_one(...)` kind of assumes all filtering will be done server-side and that endpoint will return 1 result in which we use generator to return the first result. I think it would be reasonable to have local filtering transparently if the endpoint doesn't support arguments that make this very easy, like this case.

Do you have any thoughts about that?

@nicksay, could you review, please.

Indeed imposing an order is difficult, better is to directly encode parameters of the "triangle strip":
- first you have to encode the structure of the tree. For a triangular lattice you have full binary tree - their number is Catalan number, you need ~1 bit/node: if this is a leaf or not. 
Comparing to 3x32 vertex numbers, it means 96x compression.
- then you don't need 3 float numbers per vertex - imagine such triangular lattice you want to bend - there are lots of constraints due to the fact that lengths of triangle edges have to agree. It is sufficient to store 1 length per node of the tree - e.g. length of its edge on the right (on the left is from its neighbor).
Then decoder would need to perform the bending - it is a few trigonometric operations per node - really cheap comparing to what LZMA does.

Regarding using floats - you store mostly very similar numbers - their exponent and sign (9 of 32 bits) are probably identical, also the most significant bits of the fraction.
Using fixed-precision numbers instead you should directly get like 1/2 savings.
And their most significant digits are usually from some localized probability distribution, especially if you encode differences - you can use entropy coder for them.

Just realized today that we have a bug with the way chromatic transformations revert InterpolatedChromaticObjects back to their uninterpolated versions.  There's a check in ChromaticTransformation that attempts to catch these.  It will miss though if an achromatic transformation is applied first, and then a chromatic transformation.  For example:

```
    def dilate_fn(w):
        return (w/500.0)**0.2
    gsobj = galsim.Gaussian(fwhm=1)
    chrom = galsim.ChromaticObject(gsobj).dilate(dilate_fn).interpolate(waves=[400., 700.0, 1000.0])
    caught = chrom.dilate(dilate_fn)  # This raises a warning and undoes the .interpolate in the line above.
    uncaught = chrom.shear(g1=0.1).dilate(dilate_fn)  # This does not raise the warning or undo the interpolation.
```

The best solution I've thought of so far is to just raise the warning and undo the interpolation even if the transformation is achromatic.

> Unfortunately, I cannot reliably distinguish catkin packages from other packages using rosdep. I did find the "_is_ros" key, but that is set to true for cmake build type packages as well.

This is where the `rosdistro` and the `rosdistro` cache would be useful. You could use the `rosdistro` python library to see if a rosdep is a released package and if it is you can get the `package.xml` from the cache, which would let you check to see what the `build_type` is (`cmake` versus `catkin`).

So basically a rosdep will be in one of three (and a half) categories:

- It is a rosdep key which maps to a system dependency
- It is a released ROS package
 - In this case you can check the `package.xml` for the `build_type`, `catkin` or `cmake` or something else
- It is either an invalid rosdep key (like a typo or something) or it is an unreleased package.

If it falls into the last category you can check the local system (RPP) to see if the user has it from source.

Unfortunately there isn't much in the way of documentation for the `rosdistro` python library, but you can have a look at some examples of how we use it:

- https://github.com/ros-infrastructure/rosinstall_generator
- https://github.com/ros-infrastructure/ros.org_scripts/blob/master/wiki.ros.org/indigo_metapackage_status.py
- https://github.com/ros-infrastructure/bloom/blob/master/bloom/commands/release.py#L171-274

Hopefully that gives you some more insight into the issue.

## 2. Handling entities

The new notation brings powerful feature to simplify entity casting.

### Conversions 

They are most often implicit, for instance:
```py
    # AF value casted for an individu
    af = individu.famille('af') 

    # Loyer casted to the famille entity, using the demandeur as a pivot
    loyer = famille.demandeur.menage('loyer')

    # Salaire casted to the FoyerFiscal entity, taking the value from a given role. 
    # Only works for a unique role.       
    salaire_declarant1 = foyer_fiscal.declarant_principal('salaire_net')
```

You can go pretty far with this notation, as you can see in [this test case](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/blob/refactor-entities/openfisca_france/tests/test_entities.py#L148).
         
### Aggregation
We still have two steps, but the syntax is really light.

```py
    salaire_net = famille.members('salaire_net')
    salaire_total_famille = famille.sum(salaire_net)
    salaire_max_dans_famille = famille.max(salaire_net)
    salaire_min_parents_famille = famille.min(salaire_net, role = Famille.PARENT)
    everyone_has_salary = famille.all(salaire_net > 0)
    anyone_has_salary = famille.any(salaire_net > 0)
```

Syntactic sugar like `famille.sum('salaire_net')` can be added later.

### Roles
New roles are not anymore exposed in the global scope, but attached to the corresponding entity, as you can see in the exemple above. To know if a person has a given role, use:
```py
    individu.has_role(Famille.PARENT)
```
Some roles can be included in each other:
```py
    individu.has_role(FoyerFiscal.DECLARANT)
     >>> [True, True, False, False]

    individu.has_role(FoyerFiscal.CONJOINT)
    >>> [False, True, False, False]
```
Note that unlike in the former system, there isn't a different role for the 1st child, 2nd child, etc. 


### Implementation

The constraint of staying in vectorial mode has been respected. Aggregations are based on the native `numpy.bincount`. We sometimes iterate on the roles, or on the positions in the entity, but never explicitly on the entities themselves.

This of course had a big cost and limit the possibilities. However, all has been made so that the vectorial aspect is hidden, or a least is not an obstacle to the formula writer.

This explains a lot, I ran into some similar speed issues when running synchronously. Barring a functional reason for it, @aetherknight it seems worth opening a PR to remove. 

Note: there's also a `time.sleep(1)` in `jira.JIRA.create_version`, which again introduces 1*N delays that may become apparent in large calls (for example, generating a large volume of test data via the API). The only other place `time.sleep` is used in this library is in `jira.resilientsession.ResilientSession.__recoverable`, but its use there (to enforce an exponential backoff in retrying after session failures) seems reasonable. That being the case I would expect to see a PR address both `jira.resources.Resource.update` and `jira.JIRA.create_version`. 

It would be great to get some guidance from @ssbarnea who likely has much more background on why these delays were originally required, and what the expected side effects of removing them might be.

For example, it would be useful for the purpose of removing one of the transformations such as SIP, and/or NPOL. Currently there are two ways of achieving this:

1. setting a transformation to Identity; and
2. building a new WCS from scratch.

First solution is not ideal at it leaves all these unnecessary steps in the "wcs pipeline" but it does preserve all other attributes of the WCS object: name, etc. Second solution results in a "cleaner" WCS but it requires rebuilding the wcs (which is not a one-liner) and requires copying all attributes of the original object (for now it is 'name' but in the future it may be something else and my code will have to be maintained to keep up with future changes in the GWCS).

Yeah, this is a known backend issue. Basically, it can take 2 minutes for the streaming buffer's cache of where tables are located to update, so the writes after a delete and recreate can end up going to the deleted table's files in that 2 minute window.

Related: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/issues/4553 For adding data to brand new table, it's recommended to use a load job instead. We can make doing this easier with the client library.

A fix has now been pushed to the `development` branch. 

You can install the `development` version of PyDrive using:
```bash
pip install git+https://github.com/googledrive/PyDrive.git@development#egg=PyDrive
```
Specifying optional parameters is now possible as additional parameters to the `InsertPermission()` function like so:
```python
file1.InsertPermission({'type': 'user',
                                         'value': 'insert@email.here',
                                         'role': 'reader'}, sendNotificationEmails=False)
```

Please see the official docs [here](https://developers.google.com/drive/v2/reference/permissions/insert) for all parameters available.

Been finding a bunch of false positive leads on this.

Looks to me like a race condition that is a direct result of setting the `pageState` too early - `content-page` is still getting destroyed when we activate the loading state. Pulling the `state` out from under `content-page`'s feet as its getting destroyed leads to undefined getters, including `channelId`. 

If we pre-emptively set `pageState` within `topics-page`, we get the same result in other parts of learn. 

Alltogether, it's an issue with the way the Vue lifecycle interacts with Vuex state, and we haven't seen it more often because we tend not to use `beforeDestroy()`

Since you are connecting to an IOM Workspace server, the autoexec and any other config would already be set up and configured for that workspace server. So, there's no way to pass a parameter through from the client.
Now, if you're using the Local IOM connection, then you have a local SAS install on your same PC that you're using. In that install, you have the sasv9,cfg, like the workspace server does. You configure anything like that using the normal SAS configuration. So, really it's the same as the remote configuration, but you can do it yourself (for remote, it's likely someone else that had configured those SAS sessions).
So, basically the answer is you don't do that with saspy, you do it with SAS. Same as you would do for when you just run sas.exe yourself.

This suggests that the slicing operation (line 147) is way more expensive than I thought.  What happens if you do something like [`targets.split(split_size=1, dim=1)`](http://pytorch.org/docs/master/torch.html#torch.split) above the loop to pre-slice everything, and just index into that split list?  Assuming `torch.split()` is implemented efficiently, this would be a single GPU operation instead of `num_timesteps` slice operations, which could be significantly faster. Hi,

What I would do is
A) Leave the router nodes on the Deis cluster as-is. Note that for DO to work reliable ALL nodes need a router, and DNS entries need to point to all Deis nodes. Which is fine if you have 5 nodes which run everything, or I guess  more nodes where some or routerPlane only.

B) Add 2 or more additional nodes (partially?) outside of the Deis cluster  which have a HAProxy (or nginx to be consistent?) on them which would do healtchecks on all real Deis nodes and enable/disable router nodes appropriatly.  This would make it possible to run only router nodes on 3/5 nodes and would also prevent issues when a deis node is in unstable state. [1]
The 2 or more additional nodes would have floating IPs so they can safely failover

[1] I had this situation a while ago when running out of disk space on a node, and after cleaning diskspace the docker graph was corrupted (not sure why), so it took me 10 minutes to detect and recover from this. At the same time we were having a demo to our clients, and the browser connected to this failing node, and would as a result give an error... Having some LB nodes in front of the deis cluster can prevent these issues

Hi! Thanks for coming here :-). To your questions: Yes, turbodbc can deal with strings, even large ones. There are a few caveats, though. 
* The first one is that turbodbc will allocate buffers that are sufficiently large to handle any supported value in a given column. For a `VARCHAR(4000)` that would be 4000 bytes, for a `TEXT` column that would be `2^31` bytes. This approach requires lots of memory for unrestricted columns. I have proposed a solution at #76 which I plan to implement soonish.
* Currently, turbodbc cannot deal with retrieving result sets with columns that contain `VARCHAR(MAX)`. Actually, I heard about this type from your issue :-). The thing is that ODBC drivers translate `VARCHAR(MAX)` to a `VARCHAR` type with length `0` (see [this reference](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/relational-databases/native-client/features/using-large-value-types)). Turbodbc happily uses this `0` as the actual length of the string and will allocate buffers for null termination characters. I would fix that in the same go as I fix #76, basically by passing turbodbc a maximum size that is reserved for such "unlimited" columns.

For inserting strings, turbodbc also uses a buffered approach, but the buffer size is dynamically adjusted to what you actually want to insert using an exponential strategy. So if you insert about 6000 characters, a buffer of approximately 6-8 kB will be allocated.

Regarding the Python 2.7 support, there is nothing intrinsic to turbodbc that forbids the use of Python 2.7. I continuously test it with Linux and OSX. The reason why I do not yet offer Python 2.7 support in Windows is that compiling the thing is more difficult for Python 2.7, apparently because Python 2.7 was built with an earlier compiler than I require. Once this problem is solved (don't know exactly how at the moment, but I am no Windows expert), I can release additional binary wheels. Changes should be limited to the build support files such as `appveyor.yml`, `setup.py`, and probably some `CMakeLists.txt` files.

I hope this has not scared you too much... :-)

According to the [update history](https://github.com/conda/conda/blame/master/conda/cli/install.py#L272), `force` has implied `no_deps` since January 2014. 

However, what we do know is that the dependency solver in conda 4.x has undergone a significant overhaul. This has not been without pain, caused potentially breaking changes for some people's automations. This may be related to what you are encountering.

Unfortunately, this was necessary because the 3.x solver did not consider the entire environment when installing and updating packages. Thus `conda` could _break_ environments by allowing incompatible packages to be installed. The goal with 4.x is to ensure that the only way for `conda` to break an environment in this manner is with explicit approval from the user, through the use of `--force` option.

We do have an outstanding issue involving the interaction of `--force` and `--no-deps` in this regime.

Environments are our way of avoiding environmental pollution. You should use them for that purpose. They are a good bit faster than using Anaconda with docker, but then again Miniconda would work out much faster than Anaconda on docker anyway (though you can create docker images pre baked with whatever software you need of course). Installing only what you need is a great way to save time, and with regards to environments, installing into those only what you need for your specific workload is a great way to avoid pollution.

in response to @msarahan 
> At this point, I am leaning towards ditching the whole feature concept for tracking compilers, and rather going back and doing it differently (hopefully right): track compiler used like numpy and python themselves are tracked. This was originally suggested/requested by @ukoethe in #1779 (comment)

I'm not suggesting to ditch the feature concept. To the contrary, I'd love to use it as the primary mechanism for ensuring compatible installations once the issues with feature-related version resolution (see https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1589 and references therein) are resolved.

My proposal to use jinja2 configuration is entirely orthogonal. It could be used to automatically define the appropriate features of a package. In addition, I use jinja2 to configure version suffixes, but these are mainly needed to work around the feature resolution problems mentioned above.

Hi, thanks for reporting.

The issue is that only main htslib is built when doing a pip/setup.py install, not any of the plugins.

There are two immediate solutions: 

1. build an external htslib with all the plugins you need and then use this via the HTSLIB_LIBRARY_DIR option before calling pip/setup.py. This will bypass the htslib that comes with pysam.

2. use conda, which I think comes with a plugin-enabled htslib

Longer term, I will look into what is involved building htslib plugins via setup.py. I left them out originally as I was worried that building plugins might require more exotic system libraries to be present.

@cmarqu You're right, these messages are meant to be read so they should be as readable as possible. I see a number of different cases.

* For checks like `check(en = '1', "Checking that enable is active.")` the user is in full control of the message and making sure that it becomes readable in both the pass and fail cases.
* For some checks there are actually several checks being done internally.  For example, `check_stable` checks the stability of a `std_logic[_vector]` signal between a `std_logic` start event and a `std_logic` end event. In addition to checking the stability of the signal it will also check that the event signals doesn't have X values. If that is the case it will fail with something like `"Unknown start event"`. Such a message can be changed to become readable in the pass case as well. However, I'm thinking that these types of pass messages would make the log less clean for your use case, it would be better to just output _one_ pass message for the internal check that does the actual stability check. That check uses the user provided message and the user has to make sure it becomes readable.
* In case VUnit generates a predefined error message it also let you specify your own message to be appended. So `check_equal(en, '1', "Checking that enable is active.")` would today give

   ```
ERROR: Equality check failed! Got 0. Expected 1. Checking that enable is active.
```

   If the user is responsible for writing good messages according to the first bullet, and if some messages are excluded in the pass case according to the second bullet, we might consider to make this the general approach. No internal messages are provided in the pass case, only the one provided by the user. So a passing equality check would give

   `PASS: Checking that enable is active.`

   In this case the got and expected information is known so it doesn't provide any valuable information about the context. The drawback is that you lose some context when not comparing with a known constant. I'm not sure this is the perfect solution but I'm looking for something that allow us to do this without compromising too much what we have for the most common use case of not logging pass messages.

Continuing the discussion from #429, it seems I'm at least half-right.  It's a timeout issue, but it doesn't seem to be a `master_timeout` issue (thought that may yet come into play).

Note the error: 

```
ReadTimeoutError: HTTPConnectionPool(host=u'localhost', port=9200): Read timed out. (read timeout=30)
```

This means it took longer than 30 seconds for the client to respond to Curator, so it timed out.  30 seconds is the default value.  I will change the error output from being DEBUG to ERROR so this is more visible.  

Now, the way to effectively test if this is merely a client timeout, or also a server-side `master_timeout`, is to re-run your command (keeping the `--debug` flag) with an increased `--timeout`, say 60 or 90 seconds:

```
curator --debug --timeout 60 close indices --time-unit days --older-than 30 --timestring %Y.%m.%d --prefix logstash-netflow5-
```

Should we use a one-time token for launching a browser? It's passed to the browser in a URL given as a command line argument. Command line arguments are visible to other users while the process is around, at least on Linux. So another user could see the token and use it try to connect to the same server.

I've just tested this on my system; I created a new user called `test`, logged in as that user and ran `firefox http://example.com/?foo=secret`. Switching back to my normal account, `ps -eaf | grep fire` immediately shows me the URL 'test' used.

It may be more difficult in other situations (e.g. if the browser is already running and the command opens a new tab and then exits), but we shouldn't rely on command line args staying secret. So I would have a single use token for launching a browser, and display a second token on screen for the user to copy into other browsers.

I'd like to come up with a better name for the type, though. `MixedType` is far from ideal.

With the advent of the `typing` module in Python 3.5, I've grown sympathetic to `UnionType` suggested in #340. The semantics of `typing.Union` is that of a union type, i.e., "one type from a set of *n*>=1".

Now that the concept of a union type has landed in the standard library, the default choice is to use the same nomenclature. If anyone disagrees, please voice your alternative suggestion **now**. This PR has been sitting around long enough.

As for future plans, I intend to deprecate `PolyModelType` and make its subclass resolution logic part of `ModelType` itself. Now that we have a generic union type, there's no reason for `PolyModelType` to exist.

That sounds import path related. Did you just run pip install, or did you clone the repo first? If you cloned the repo, ran pip install, then try to import from the same checkout directory, Python's going to try to import first from the local package rather than the installed version in site-packages (and the local package hasn't compiled the C libs, so it will error). If that's what happened, you can still import from your local checkout by running ```python setup.py build_ext --inplace```.

If that's not it, it may be related to the use of sudo.  I haven't used CentOS in a few years, so my information is slightly out of date, but as I recall CentOS has been slow to adopt newer Python versions, which often created conflicts between system python and the version used by applications. If that's still the case, you might try creating a virtualenv, activating and then doing a sudo-less ```pip install postal```.

I keep hitting speed issues with dask due to cloudpickling large-ish objects. These objects are fairly trivial for the C pickler but take minutes for CloudPickle, and it's making an otherwise interactive analysis a bit of a pain. Two key things I want to pickle:

1. A `TfidfVectorizer.transform` function, which comes with a couple of large numpy arrays.
1. A set with millions of strings in it (to use with a `ddf.column.isin(s)` operation in distributed dask)

I did my own digging. It that the fundamental problem is that the [C pickler's dispatch table is only consulted](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/733d0f63c562090a2b840859b96028d6ec0a4803/Modules/_pickle.c#L3868-L3869) after the fundamental types (including function) [have already been written](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/733d0f63c562090a2b840859b96028d6ec0a4803/Modules/_pickle.c#L3823-L3858).

The "obvious" fix would be to move this lookup to before the fundamental types, assuming that it is not too expensive to do this lookup all the time. This would then allow inserting things into the C pickler's dispatch table such as function. @pitrou have you considered such a thing? I assume that lookup is likely relatively cheap. Clearly such a move would be a trade-off with a (possibly small?) negative impact on other types of pickling operations. But maybe it's worth it to enable much more efficient pickling of other types of things.

I also [threw together a hack](https://gist.github.com/pwaller/86c24fadf6a514416f7d22f9cac107d8) which enables cloudpickle to efficiently pickle large sets. For my 1M element set, it gives a 30x speedup over the pure python pickler. I assume works with numpy arrays too.

The basic reason hacks of this style can't be used in general is that once you go into the C side, you then can't come back to the python side. That's fine to do if you know your containers don't have any objects in them which will need cloudpickle.

Hi Sasank,
thank you for your feedback. There are a couple of ways in which you can split an experiment into different files. A very basic hack is to import a function into your experiment file and turn it into a captured function. Something like this: 
``` python
from utils import my_function
my_function = ex.capture(my_function)
```

But of course this won't lead to well modularized code, since the configuration for `my_function` is still part of your experiment file and you will need to keep the naming consistent in both files. 

If I understand your usecase correctly, it is precisely what I intended [`Ingredients`](https://sacred.readthedocs.org/en/latest/ingredients.html) to solve. At this point they are not very well documented and a bit beta, because I'm still figuring out how exactly they should behave. 

The basic idea of an `Ingredient` is to be a reusable part of an experiment. You can define them in separate file, and they have their own configuration and captured functions. But they can be added to an experiment and then their configuration will be part of the experiment config. 
Maybe you could give them a shot and see how they work for you. I'd be very happy to get some feedback on them, so please let me know if they solve your problem and about any issues or unexpected behaviours you encounter.  Thank you.

@NikolajBjorner Regarding the `set_next_arg` warning I think http://stackoverflow.com/questions/18515183/c-overloaded-virtual-function-warning-by-clang does a reasonable job explaining the issue.

My understanding is that the `exec_given_tatic_cmd::virtual void set_next_arg(cmd_context & ctx, sexpr * arg)` declaration hides (i.e. they are not inherited) all the `set_next_arg` declarations from the parent class `parametric_cmd`.

Clang's warning is useful here because frequently that is a mistake. The stackoverflow posts suggests two solutions

* Add a `using parametric_cmd::set_next_arg` declaration into `exec_given_tactic_cmd` to make it explicit that you want to inherit the overloads of the parent class.

* Use a pragma to suppress that warning if this is really the behaviour that is intended

I don't know that code so I don't know what is intended here but I suspect that adding a `using parametric_cmd::set_next_arg` declaration is the right thing to do here.

I have noticed that softmax + cross entropy loss does result create instability in any MLP that uses rectified units. I suspect the softmax units are blowing up because the gradient mathematically simplifies to (sofmax_unit_i - target_i). See the last equation in http://www.willamette.edu/~gorr/classes/cs449/classify.html for reference.

I haven't checked if theano is actually doing the correct simplification for the gradient. But assuming it is, the softmax seems like a culprit for creating NaNs since its exponential w.r.t to the previous layer activations  (particularly bad with unbounded activation that @jsusskin mentioned). 

One easy fix that I tend to use now is either switching to HingeLoss or putting a batch normalization layer right before the softmax helps. Batch normalization actually fits quite well with the XCov loss too because it turns the XCov loss into something more akin to an XCorr loss.

> But that doesn't make clear it's going to regularize the full network. People might inadvertently use it as 0.005 * l2_weight_decay(l_out) + 0.001 * l2_weight_decay(l_hid). (I guess that's about what happened to you?)

I think this argument is being given too much weight. `get_output()` also does not explicitly indicate that it gets the output of the network, not the individual layer (or layers) that you pass to it. It just does that by convention. By this line of reasoning we would have to rename this function as well.

It's nice to have clear and descriptive names, but it's a trade-off and there is a point where it just becomes too cumbersome. I think `l2_weight_decay` would be fine, but I'd rather have a shortcut where i can just specify `l2` or `l1` or whatever else we come up with as a parameter. As our design goals state:

> Pragmatism: making common use cases easy is more important than supporting every possible use case out of the box.

I think pragmatism trumps clarity here. We need this shortcut, just because we can't come up with a descriptive name for it is not a good enough reason not to have it.

I had one for exactly the same reason :) We always wanted to add that, the major point holding us back was that nobody came up with a good name. I think `NonlinearityOnlyLayer` isn't too bad. What do the others think? (Yay, a name discussion!)

> My goal is just to obtain the value of the first parameter

`found.state` has all the information you need. If `found.state.ip` corresponds to the call instruction (contrast to atoi's plt stub or the function itself), then you can access the first parameter via `found.state.se.any_int(found.state.stack_read(offset=0, length=4))`. If you are inside the function already use `offset=4` instead. If 64 bit, use `found.state.se.any_int(found.state.regs.rdi)`

There is more than one way to obtain that information (e.g., stepping into the call and using `SimCC` as @rhelmot suggested, or modifying the atoi `SimProcedure`). If this way isn't sufficient for your use case, feel free to follow up with more information.

from rqalpha.api import buy_open, subscribe, get_order, ORDER_STATUS, POSITION_EFFECT, SIDE

def handle_bar(context, bar_dict):
    order_id = buy_open(context.f1, 1)
    order = get_order(order_id)
    assert order.order_book_id == context.f1
    assert order.quantity == 1
    assert order.status == ORDER_STATUS.ACTIVE

Hi all,

Thanks for getting this started, but I think this requires a redesign. Not all featurizers should be distance metrics, and not all distance metrics should be featurizers. e.g.

- a featurizer that returns a class as string (e.g., main orbital component of VBM like "Fe_d")  wouldn't work with a distance function at all. 
- Featurizers that return a single float aren't really great as distance metrics (although they should technically "work")
- Some distance metrics have no good featurizer counterpart. For example, let's say someone wants to define the distance between two Compositions as the percent of elements they have in common. That's a valid (if simplistic) distance metric, but there's no "featurizer" equivalent.

Thus, these two interfaces should be kept separate. I'll think about it more but my current suggestion is:

- keep base featurizer as-is
- add a new distance metric base class
- add a new class called DistanceFeaturizer (or something) that extends both base featurizer and base distance metric, and can contain some kind of built-in automatic distance computation.

I also haven't looked much at the details of the distance functions. In particular, there's a lot in scipy (https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/spatial.distance.html) and I would likely advocate just leveraging that.

> (1) Module call approach: this has already been tried with [this branch](https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/compare/master...danuzclaudes:2.9-issue-setupmodule).

This approach is feasible, just not with the specific approach listed in that branch. In that branch, repositories are created and synced in `setUpModule()` functions and destroyed in `tearDownModule()` functions. That's totally OK; what's problematic is to store these module-wide repository IDs in `pulp_smash/tests/rpm/cli/utils.py`. What happens if two different modules are executed in parallel? Two different `setUpModule()` functions will write their repository ID to `pulp_smash.tests.rpm.cli.utils.REPO_ID`, and one of the values will be overwritten & lost.

A more work-able approach is to store the repository ID referenced by `setUpModule()` and `tearDownModule()` in the module, alongside the functions. For example, `pulp_smash.tests.rpm.cli.utils.test_foo.setUpModule()` might write to `pulp_smash.tests.rpm.cli.utils.test_foo._REPO_ID`, and `pulp_smash.tests.rpm.cli.utils.test_bar.setUpModule()` might write to `pulp_smash.tests.rpm.cli.utils.test_bar._REPO_ID`. This approach allows for a single repository to be shared by an entire module.

This still isn't ideal, as we've now restricted ourselves to parallelising tests at the per-module level. Tests can't be parallelised at the per-test-case level. :( But it at least allows some modules to sync less data.

> (2) Inheritance approach:
>
> Create a `rpm.cli.BaseTestCase`; each test module is a subclass of it, and each calls the method `super.create_sync_global_repo()` which will create its own "global" repository of the module. Please let me know which is the better option here.

Classes can use inheritance. Modules can not.

----

If your intent is to make Pulp Smash more efficient, so that it syncs less data, what you're really asking for is for the unittest framework itself to create fixtures at some level higher than the class level. unittest offers [class and module fixtures](https://docs.python.org/3.5/library/unittest.html#class-and-module-fixtures); unittest does not offer package fixtures. It may be possible to shoe-horn in package-level fixtures by creating a module such as `pulp_smash.tests.rpm.cli.test_fixtures`, which would create and sync a repository.

Before going down this route, though, it's worth asking:

* Will this cause issues with future development work? (Possibly. Test case parallelisation doesn't play well with module-wide or package-wide fixtures.)
* Is this the best way to make the test suite execute faster? (We don't know. We need to add coverage reports to find out what our test suite is even spending time doing.)
* Is this the most important goal for us right now? (No. We need to port RCM tests. We need coverage reports on Pulp; we need to test Pulp & Satellite together; we need to port RCM tests; etc.)

Hi @bigblind,
`connection_from_list` expect an iterable (this iterable had to implement `len` and a item getter like `iterable[20:30]` though). Could be a `list`, could be a lazy list, or anything that you could iter through.
However, we don't iter through the iterable until the end (when we show the results).

In the example of [Starwars relay](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/blob/master/examples/starwars_relay/schema.py) the `resolve_ships` return a complete list. But the [swapi example](https://github.com/graphql-python/swapi-graphene/blob/master/starwars/schema.py#L146) the method returns an iterable.

So, for example, when you are using django querysets, the list is only "resolved" (aka queried in the DB) when we iter though it (when we return the resolved result), and after the list is sliced.

So it will already do in the most optimal way :)

Ok, so this is a tricky design decision in some ways because we want to mimic the "argument magic" that javascript can manage in python. We've also got a possible name clash issue.

Here are a few possible approaches:
- Use decorators to explicitly mark which resolvers need which arguments. This is the current proposal. It doesn't feel very pythonic. We could have a convention that `context` and `info` could be passed as `_context` and `_info` if there's a name clash, or always.
- Use the inspect module to find the signature. `inspect.getargspec(resolver_func).args` will return a list of names of arguments. If that includes `context` or `info` then we pass them. This is "fully" magical, but I don't find it any more distasteful than the `_root`/`getattr` pattern we have at the moment. I'm not sure if we can get access to which arguments might be relevant to deal with name clashes, but it would be easy to see if a user is expecting `context` AND `_context` and pass the arg as the former and the context as the latter.
- Define a more pythonic standard API for a resolver. Something like `def resolve_foo(self, arg1, arg2, **options)`. Again we can deal with name clashes with `_context` if necessary, or just have a convention that you don't do that. I feel this is the route we would be taking if there wasn't the JS reference implementation.

# Proposal: The Custom Scalars Plugin

## Overview
We propose a plugin (short name `custom-scalars`) that lets the user make several custom scalar plots. The lines shown in each plot will depend on

- The runs selected on the left hand side (per the practice in other plugins).
- The regex for filtering tags specific to each plot.

The need for this plugin is noted both above as well as in #597 - users would like to customize which tags appear in individual plots. Currently, the scalars dashboard only shows 1 plot per tag. Several researchers would like to try out this plugin.

## Implementation Details

### Dashboard Design

The dashboard will show a "+ Add Plot" button at the top. When the user clicks it, an empty chart is added along with a regex input field on top of it. When the user inputs a regex, the chart shows lines for tags that are captured by the regex. A legend below clarifies which tags are captured. Tags within the same run are differentiated by different markers. Runs are differentiated by colors as expected.

A low-fidelity paper prototype:

![img_20171002_160521](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4221553/31103674-14ea1096-a78d-11e7-90aa-e13490a24eb2.jpg)

As you see, this plugin does not rely on runs for categorization of plots.

### Frontend Implementation

We refactor the `tf-line-chart-data-loader` component to accept multiple tags:
https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/d1a5098aa2884b6b8e6194f30f4138e8ee2be2c7/tensorboard/components/tf_line_chart_data_loader/tf-line-chart-data-loader.html#L114

We refactor `vz-line-chart` to assign a new marker shape whenever the same color is used more than once (which should probably already be the case). 
https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/d1a5098aa2884b6b8e6194f30f4138e8ee2be2c7/tensorboard/components/vz_line_chart/vz-line-chart.ts#L669

For each plot, we pass a special color scale from the plugin to `tf-line-chart-data-loader` that assigns the same color for each run. And then, we construct each data series name by passing in the name of the tag suffixed by ` (run: foo)`. The data series name will no longer be just the name of the run.

### Backend Design

The constructor of the `custom-scalars` plugin will take a `scalars` plugin and use the latter to serve specific scalars data to the frontend. This is similar to how the distributions plugin takes a histograms plugin.

#### Routes

`/tags`: This route simply returns a JSON list of all tags with scalar data. The frontend will apply regexes to that list.
`/scalar_data?run=foo&tag=bar`: This route returns a list of scalar data given a run and tag. This route will be called several times by the frontend depending on how many tags a user's regex captures.

OK, new proposal: Lets try out 1 global slider for all runs. The advantage is that we can conveniently specify 1 step cap for all runs. The disadvantage is that runs can differ in sampling rate of summaries as well as max step, so if we rely on 1 global slider alone, some steps might become inaccessible to certain runs.

Imagine a run A at step 10K and another run B at step 1K. TensorBoard samples for 10 events per run (or tag really, but assume all tags share the same steps here). With 1 global slider sampled at the rate of run A, we can access all sampled images of run A, but only 2 of run B.

Hence, how about we try 1 global slider but also maintain the individual slider per image. @wchargin, thoughts?

I have a hunch that precision here will turn out to be important when bringing this to IETF. People with all kinds of different ideas about how operating systems can implement IPv6 nodes by offloading some of their interface functions to co-processor hardware will be reviewing this document. Hopefully, we can avoid a lot of semantic confusion when they all start talking at once.

To be precise with the RFC 4007 text, a link-local unicast zone index (link-local has since become the only kind of unicast zone index, though there are still multiple kinds of multicast zone indices) is associated within an IPv6 node to a specific IPv6 link. Every interface has a link-local unicast IPv6 address, and the zone index is used within the scope of the node as an identifier for the link.

I suppose renaming this field "zone index number" might be a point of confusion because it could leave people wondering about the distinction between unicast and multicast zone indices.

How about "network link index" (NLI) instead?

@lvdmaaten I moved the downloading of scripts to server.py and executed before launching the server. This means we don't have access rights to copy the files to visdom installation directory. Here is an example converting from a dask bag to a dask array of sparse arrays using dask.delayed:

```python
import dask.bag as db
import sparse
b = db.from_sequence(range(10), npartitions=3)

def f(partition):
    n = sum(partition)
    return sparse.random((n, 5), density=0.2)

f([0, 1, 2, 3])
# <COO: shape=(6, 5), dtype=float64, nnz=6>

delayed_values = b.map_partitions(f).to_delayed()
delayed_values[0].compute()
# <COO: shape=(6, 5), dtype=float64, nnz=6>

import numpy as np
import dask.array as da
arrays = [da.from_delayed(dv, shape=(np.nan, 5), dtype=float) for dv in delayed_values]
x = da.concatenate(arrays)
x
# dask.array<concatenate, shape=(nan, 5), dtype=float64, chunksize=(nan, 5)>

x.compute()
# <COO: shape=(45, 5), dtype=float64, nnz=45>
```

Note that we use `np.nan` in a shape when we don't know the number of elements along an axis of an array.

I'd rather recommend using task management app that is "native" to the VTODO format. Converting from VTODO to the XML-based format (and back) will probably not be lossless.

Of course I would accept a PR that implements this. You will need the `icalendar` library for parsing VTODOs (vdirsyncer's internal parser is very primitive). Please add that as new line in `extras_require` in `setup.py`. `xml.etree` from the stdlib will probably suffice for generating the tudu file.

The abstract interface is documented in `vdirsyncer/storage/base.py`. Each file in that folder (other than `base.py`) represents an actual storage type you can use. A very simple one is `memory.py` which stores all items in memory. The second most simple one is `filesystem.py`. Those are useful for getting a hang of the API you need to implement, but you will want to take a look at `singlefile.py` too.

So what you need to do is to create a subclass of `vdirsyncer.storage.base.Storage` and override all of its methods. You then also need to create a new `test_*.py` file in `tests/storage` that tests this new storage.

Finally you need to register that new storage in a table at https://github.com/pimutils/vdirsyncer/blob/ad8fc1dc75d931708c0c80596c0a72199906f6df/vdirsyncer/cli/utils.py#L39-L51

For an example PR take a look at #500, though that is unfinished.

Feel free to ask questions. We have an IRC channel on freenode called `#pimutils`. It isn't logged, even though there are no stupid questions :)

Just sat down and sketched out an approach with Erica. Basically amounts to…

* a tox config with pinned shallow versions of the minimum allowed dep versions
* installing the certbot packages with `--no-deps`
* declaring deps like `>=1.1.4,<=2.0` in the setup.py files

Then we'd have a second tox env that tests the latest versions of everything together, because that's a common real-world setup. We should probably just let pip do its thing for that rather than trying to keep up with a pinning; we will *want* to know when somebody does a release of a dep that makes things go bad.

Unfortunately, Certbot doesn't exactly follow the Linux FHS. While I don't think there is a single directory that is standard for lockfiles across all UNIX systems (see [FreeBSD](https://www.freebsd.org/cgi/man.cgi?query=hier&sektion=7) for instance), you could argue that putting the lockfile somewhere in `/var` is standard. We had problems with this which are explained at the bottom of https://github.com/certbot/certbot/pull/4449#issuecomment-292578050.

While I'm sorry for the trouble and would be open to changing this in the future, I don't think we want to break backwards compatibility by moving the lockfile to another location right now.

> The `--cert-name` flag can also be used to modify the domains a certificate contains, by specifying new domains using the `-d` or `--domains` flag.

To me, that means things I list after `-d` are added to the certificate.

I realize now that this is not at all how either`--expand` and `--cert-name` actually works.  If I understand correctly, you *always* have to re-list all domains if you want to keep them covered by the cert.

Here's two things that can be done to help:

1. Make sure that this fact, that `-d` *overwrites all* domains on the cert, is very clear in the docs.
2. Add a new flag to add domains to a named cert without removing old domains.  I would suggest `--expand`, but I understand the desire to keep the behavior stable.

I have been working on Implementing the Content Item Specification for my application and wanted to run a few things by people as they pertain to LTI 2 as well.

The Content Item Specification as well as much of LTI 2 makes use of JsonLD an extension to JSON. There are two python libraries that implement the extension.

https://github.com/digitalbazaar/pyld

https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib-jsonld

pyLD seems to be more pythonic and has no other dependencies while rdflib-jsonld is dependent on rdflib, so my recommendation is that we use pyLD. I was hoping that the use of JSON LD would be fairly trivial and we could just build json documents with @ signs in a few places and be good, but after looking through tome of the LTI 2 documentation they do make use of some of the Json LD  features.

The other thing i wanted to bring up related to LTI 2 is that there are a number of new message types, currently the LaunchParams class validates the keys globally, it may be a good idea to change that to validate based on the message type.

Using number based tags is error prone. It is very easy to write proper comment and put wrong number (i.e. 0x0300 instead of 0x0030). Then it would be hard to debug whether proper tag was used or not.

I'd suggest to stick to official standarized tag names, saved as last items of tuple here
https://github.com/darcymason/pydicom/blob/master/pydicom/_dicom_dict.py

These names are extracted by pydicom from official DICOM standard XML files, see:
https://github.com/darcymason/pydicom/blob/master/source/generate_dict/generate_dicom_dict.py#L197

Checking if tag is present could also be done in more elegant way than by `hasattr`, i.e:

```
if 'ROIContours' in dcm.dir():
   ...
elif ...
```

See https://pydicom.readthedocs.io/en/stable/pydicom_user_guide.html describing usage of `dir()` method.

Central question is:
- can we have **two different tag** names with **same** tag-id, which will result in **two different attributes** set upon dicom import?

That was the feeling I and @okejdokej got when debugging the Pinnacle import issue. And our conclusion was it would be more safe to refer by tag-id instead of attribute name.

It remains unconfirmed though.

This is not as such a bug in QCoDeS.

The problem is that some instruments, the Keithley for instance, do not handle dead/multiple connections very well. That means that the user *must* make sure to explicitly close any connection made to that instrument, or new connection will not be accepted. The fix to ensure that this always happens is to use the `atexit` module to close all QCoDeS `Instrument` instances. This is done in `__init__.py`, here and the next line: https://github.com/QCoDeS/Qcodes/blob/ac88bd6f7071fc0861fa3cbf2866f3a6e0db1bb8/qcodes/__init__.py#L93

So, yeah, I guess this issue is resolved?

On Linux, the best way to assure this is by setting `pdeathsig` on a parent process. Then if the parent process dies, Linux kills also all children. In Python you can do this by adding to `subprocess` `Popen` call arguments:

```
preexec_fn=lambda: prctl.set_pdeathsig(signal.SIGKILL)
```

You [need this package](https://pythonhosted.org/python-prctl/).
